---
mode: ask
description: "Use when: user wants a big-picture refresh, scope reminder, or alignment check before implementation."
---
Read and apply the project big-picture context from PROJECT_SCOPE.md.

Then output exactly these sections:
1. Current Mission
- Our goal is to develop Whitebox Next Gen, which is the latest evolution of the Whitebox Project. There are two legacy projects from which this is derived, WhiteboxTools (programming/Rust/whitebox-tools and programming/Rust/wbt-plugins) and Whitebox Workflows for Python (WbW, contained within programming/Rust/whitebox_workflows). Whitebox Next Gen will replace both of these and represents a major re-write of the entire platform. The project README.md (programming/Rust/whitebox_next_gen/README.md) contains a high-level overview of the project, and the PROJECT_SCOPE.md contains detailed information about the mission, design goals, non-negotiables, scope boundaries, and other important context for the project. Importantly, the project is being developed in a way where the core functionality is open-source and contained within the whitebox_next_gen repository (the "monorepo"), while a Pro tier with additional features and capabilities is being developed in a separate repository (wbtools_pro) as a closed-core extension of the platform. The project is focused on creating a powerful, flexible, and user-friendly geospatial analysis platform that can be used by a wide range of users, from researchers to practitioners, and that can support a variety of use cases and applications in the geospatial domain. 
- Among the most important design goals for the Whitebox project (which you'll find in the monorepo README and PROJECT_SCOPE.md) are:
  - Broad geospatial functionality with deep specialization
  - Full-stack architecture
  - Pure Rust, minimal dependencies (for the core platform, not the frontends)
- The legacy versions of Whitebox were developed as large monolithic codebases that included a wide range of functionality, but were difficult to maintain and extend over time. Whitebox Next Gen is being developed as a more modular and flexible platform that can be easily extended and maintained over time, while still providing a powerful and user-friendly experience for users. With this in mind, the project defines a number of core "backend" crates for doing things like raster, vector, and lidar I/O, handling projections, and performing core vector topology ops.  
- Additionally, the project contains a number of core "frontends", which are user-facing interfaces that build on top of the backend crates to provide specific functionality and user experiences. The open-core or standard tier of the platform is contained within the monorepo. These equal frontends include the Python and R APIs (wbw_python and wbw_r) and a QGIS plugin (wbw_qgis). Each of these must be maintained as though they are equal priorities and have an equivalent set of functionalities.

2. Non-Negotiables In Force
- Whitebox Next Gen's backend crates must be pure Rust and use external crates extremely sparingly, always preferring in-house implementations when possible. This is to ensure that the core of the platform is open-source, maintainable, and not dependent on external libraries that may have their own licensing or maintenance issues.
- The Pro tier must be developed as a closed-core extension of the platform, with additional features and capabilities that build on top of the open-core functionality contained within the monorepo. This means that the Pro tier can include features that are not open-source, but it must still be designed in a way that is compatible with the open-core and does not compromise the integrity or maintainability of the core platform.
- The project must be developed in a way that is focused on creating a powerful, flexible, and user-friendly geospatial analysis platform that can be used by a wide range of users, from researchers to practitioners, and that can support a variety of use cases and applications in the geospatial domain. This means that the design and implementation of the platform must prioritize usability, flexibility, and functionality, and must be guided by the needs and requirements of the target user base.
- Always build wbw_python, wbw_r, and wbw_qgis with Pro enabled when doing platform integration/testing.
- Keep RuntimeSession catalog, taxonomy TOML, and frontend exports in sync.
- We should never modify the files contained within the legacy Whitebox project folders (Rust/whitebox_workflows, Rust/whitebox-tools, Rust/wbt-plugins) unless I have explicitly ask you to do so.
- Whever we modify any of the backend crates (wbgeotiff, wbprojection, wbraster, wbvector, wblidar, and wbtopology), we must update their changelog files. These crates are now published on crates.io, so it is important to maintain accurate and up-to-date changelogs for each of these crates to ensure that users can easily see what changes have been made in each release and to maintain the integrity of the open-source project.
- Any modification of the backend crates that you think necessitates a new dependency must be explicitly approved by me before you proceed with implementation. This is to ensure that we maintain the principle of minimal dependencies and that any new dependencies are carefully considered and justified before being added to the project.
- When compiling crates, we need to ensure that all of the features of the backend crates are enabled. This includes ensuring that they use full parallelism. This is important for testing and development to ensure that we are using the full capabilities of the backend crates and that we are not inadvertently introducing performance issues or other problems by using a limited set of features during development.
- The backend crates are all published to crates.io because they are intended to be open-source and available for use by the wider community as a series of foundational Rust-based geospatial libraries. However, the remaining crates in the monorepo will not be published to crates.io because they are intended to be part of the overall Whitebox Next Gen platform and are not designed to be used as standalone libraries. This means that while the backend crates can be used independently by other developers, the remaining crates in the monorepo are intended to be used as part of the overall platform and are not designed for independent use.
- The legacy version of WbW (programming/Rust/whitebox_workflows) is the benchmark for comparing the performance of Whitebox Next Gen tools and API constructs. Any observable slowing down of code is a regression.
- Tools should be implemented in the Python API (wbw_python) using their categorized method, that is we should prefer wbe.category.tool() over the flat method of wbe.tool() whenever possible. Flat methods should be reserved for non-tool functionality.
- Whenever we modify a tool, the Python stub file signature must be updated. We want to have rich type informaiton in the stubs to support users in IDEs and to provide a clear contract for frontend implementations. This is especially important for tools that have complex parameter sets or that are used frequently by users, as it can help to reduce errors and improve the overall user experience when working with the Python API. Tools with input string options should have those options reflected in the stub file signature as literal types whenever possible, to provide even richer type information and to help users understand the expected inputs for those parameters. There is a script (scripts/rollout_stub_literals.py) that handles some of this for us.
- For tool testing activities, you should perform optimizations and run cargo check, but leave full relase-mode builds and benchmarks to me. This is to ensure that we maintain a consistent approach to testing and optimization, and that we are not inadvertently introducing performance issues or other problems by running full release-mode builds during development. By leaving full release-mode builds and benchmarks to me, we can ensure that we are maintaining a high level of quality and performance for the platform while still allowing for efficient development and testing during the implementation phase.
- Please don't create git branches. I am only one programmer working on this project and I want to keep the commit history clean and linear. If you need to pause work on a particular task or feature, you can simply stop working on it and move on to something else without creating a separate branch. This will help to maintain a clear and concise commit history that accurately reflects the development process and the changes that have been made to the project over time. I will always work on main. If for some reason in the future we absolutely need to create a new branch for some work, you have to explicitly ask me for permission to do so before you proceed. This is to ensure that we maintain a consistent approach to version control and that we are not inadvertently introducing complexity or confusion into the commit history by creating unnecessary branches.

3. In-Scope Systems And Files
- Whitebox Next Gen consists of two main components: 1. the open-core, which is contained within the whitebox_next_gen repository (the "monorepo"), and is the standard or open tier of the platform, and 2. the Pro tier, which is a closed-core extension of the platform that includes additional features and capabilities. The Pro tier is contained within the wbtools_pro repository, which is separate from the monorepo. The monorepo includes all the core functionality of Whitebox Next Gen, while the Pro tier builds on top of that core with additional tools and features.
- The Pro tier crate (wbtools_pro) includes more than 40 "workflow" style tools. These are quite different than the single-function tools that are contained within the monorepo, in that they are designed to perform more complex, multi-step analyses that build on top of the core functionality contained within the monorepo. These workflow tools are designed to provide more advanced capabilities and features for users who need them, while still being compatible with the open-core functionality contained within the monorepo.
- The monorepo includes wbgeotiff, wbprojection, wbraster, wbvector, wblidar, and wbtopology crates, which are the core backend crates for doing things like raster, vector, and lidar I/O, handling projections, and performing core vector topology ops. These crates must be pure Rust and use external crates extremely sparingly, always preferring in-house implementations when possible.
- The frontend crates, also contained within the monorepo, include wbw_python, wbw_r, and wbw_qgis, which are the user-facing interfaces that build on top of the backend crates to provide specific functionality and user experiences. 
- There are a number of other crates, such as wbcore, wblicense_core, which provide orchestration and shared functionality across the platform, and which are also contained within the monorepo.
- While it is not currently an active part of the Whitebox Next Gen platform, we have previously developed a crate called wbphotogrammetry, which has since been removed from the platform. This was a failed attempt to create a complete photogrammetry pipeline. There are parts of that crate that have been salvaged to provide some of the functionality for the Pro tier workflow tools.
- The mechanism in place to ensure that each of the three frontends (Python, R, and QGIS) have an equivalent set of functionalities is to maintain a shared register of tools and tool categories/subcategories within the tool_taxonomy.toml file. This file is used by a script (sync_tool_taxonomy.py) to ensure the frontends see the same set of tools and categories. Whenever we add a new tool or make changes to the tool taxonomy, we must update this file and run the script to ensure that the frontends are all aligned in terms of the tools and categories they support. This is an important part of maintaining the consistency and usability of the platform across different user interfaces.
- There was a "bridge" crate in the monorepo called wbjpeg2000. It was a vendored version of a third party crate for reading/writing JPEG2000 files. We used it to study while we implemented native support for JPEG2000 within the wbraster crate. This crate has subsequently been removed from the project and the bridge torn down.

4. Immediate Goal (from user request)

Rules:
- If PROJECT_SCOPE.md is missing or stale, ask for missing details first.
- If user request conflicts with PROJECT_SCOPE.md, call out the conflict explicitly.
- Keep output concise and implementation-focused.
